titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to William J.H. Tyrrell 17, October, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell is in a very remote area with no houses. He wonders if the post will be slower and suspects some parcels have "gone west".

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Saturday 17th Oct 17 My dear Daddy There is really very little news that I
can tell you. We are in an absolutely
desolate part of the world. We are in
quite a nice camp and are quite
comfortable and warm which is the great
thing. I was delighted to get your
parcel of 10th yesterday. I think tho' that
some parcels must have gone West or
in any case been considerably
delayed. however they may all come
in time. So far my efforts to find Garrett have
been no good. I have not even
found his company but I got
information tonight which may
lead me to were he is. I hope so
any way but sure he may be on
leave by now. We have a lot of new officers and
men, many of whom I knew before.
The weather is quite respectable. We
have not had any rain for some
days. I got no letters from you yesterday
or today but no doubt they will
all come in a bunch. I scribled a line in a great hurry
to you today to catch the post as
I was just going out. I don't know if my letters will
take longer now but I should
not think so. However time
will tell that. I wrote Bobby a line today
too as I had not written to
in for 3 or 4 days. The only thing really peculiar about
this part of the world is that there
is not a single house anywhere
standing. Parts of the country
all the trees were cut too but we
are on the edge of a big wood. I will write you as long a letter
as possible again soon but there
is no news here as we never see
anyone to get any news from. With best love to all
Your most affec Son
Willie
